title: Implementation Note issued May 4, 2022
summary: The Federal Reserve Board voted unanimously on May 4, 2022 to raise the interest rate paid on reserve balances to 0.9% and implement the FOMC's 50 basis point rate hike, establishing a federal funds target range of 0.75% to 1.0%. The Implementation Note specifies the operational parameters including overnight repo operations with a $500 billion aggregate limit at a 1.0% minimum bid rate and overnight reverse repo operations at a 0.8% offering rate with a $160 billion per-counterparty limit.
key_points: Federal funds rate target range raised to 0.75% to 1.0%, effective May 5, 2022
Interest rate on reserve balances raised to 0.9%, effective May 5, 2022
Overnight repurchase agreement operations: minimum bid rate 1.0%, aggregate limit $500 billion (expandable at Chair's discretion)
Overnight reverse repurchase agreement operations: offering rate 0.8%, per-counterparty limit $160 billion per day (expandable at Chair's discretion)
Treasury securities reinvestment cap: $30 billion per month (June), thereafter rolling over principal payments at auction
Agency MBS reinvestment cap: $17.5 billion per month (June), with modest operational deviations allowed
Primary credit rate increased 0.5 percentage points to 1.0%, effective May 5, 2022
why_it_matters: This Implementation Note documents the operational execution of the Fed's May 2022 rate hike, which was the largest single meeting increase since 2000. It provides the specific operational parameters—including the $500 billion repo cap and $160 billion reverse repo per-counterparty limits—that define how monetary policy translates into market mechanics. These parameters directly affect short-term funding markets and the distribution of reserves across the banking system.
macro_context: The May 2022 rate hike represented a significant acceleration in the Fed's tightening cycle, responding to elevated inflation (CPI at 8.3% in April 2022). The 50 bps move exceeded the 25 bps guidance provided at the March 2022 meeting, reflecting the FOMC's shift toward front-loading rate increases. This was part of a broader pivot from near-zero rates maintained during the pandemic emergency.
market_structure_context: The Implementation Note details the operational framework: the $500 billion repo operation limit constrains the Fed's ability to add reserves through repo operations; the $160 billion per-counterparty reverse repo limit caps the amount banks can park at the Fed's reverse repo facility. Treasury reinvestment caps at $30 billion/month and MBS caps at $17.5 billion/month define the pace of balance sheet composition changes. The 1.0% repo minimum bid rate sits 25 bps above the top of the fed funds target range.
notes: Historical record: published May 4, 2022. This is the operational implementation note following the May 4, 2022 FOMC statement. The document is authoritative Federal Reserve documentation. Source is a press release from the Federal Reserve Board of Governors website. The June 2022 reinvestment caps ($30bn Treasury, $17.5bn MBS) were temporary monthly caps that would increase in subsequent months as QT phase-in progressed. Confirmed as archiveable content type (press release, not homepage/navigation/listing/search page).
